eli3: Causal inference is the science of estimating the effect of an intervention (cause) on an outcome, not just predicting their association. Correlation does not imply causation: countries with more TVs have lower infant mortality, but buying TVs won't save babies (wealth is the common cause). Causal inference methods distinguish the causal effect of X on Y from the confounded observed association.

The potential outcomes framework (Rubin) and structural causal models (Pearl's DAGs) are the two main formalisms. Both answer the question: what is the average difference in outcome if everyone received treatment versus if everyone received control? The challenge is fundamental: we only observe one potential outcome per unit — the one that actually occurred.

When RCTs are infeasible, quasi-experimental methods estimate causal effects from observational data by exploiting specific sources of variation that mimic randomization: instrumental variables (IV) use an exogenous shock, difference-in-differences (DiD) uses panel data with a treatment timing discontinuity, regression discontinuity (RDD) exploits a cutoff rule, and propensity score matching adjusts for measured confounders.

whenWhy.title: Estimating policy effects from observational data
whenWhy.body: Government policies cannot be randomly assigned — we can't randomly assign people to minimum wage levels, education policies, or tax rates. DiD, RDD, and IV use policy variation as natural experiments. Example: Card & Krueger (1994) used DiD with New Jersey's minimum wage increase (treatment) vs Pennsylvania (control) to estimate the effect on employment, finding no negative effect — contradicting standard economic theory.

whenWhy.title: Difference-in-differences: exploiting policy timing
whenWhy.body: DiD: compare the change in outcome for the treated group (before and after treatment) to the change for the control group (same period). The DiD estimator is: (treated post-treatment average minus treated pre-treatment average) minus (control post-treatment average minus control pre-treatment average). Identifying assumption: parallel trends — treated and control groups would have followed the same trend in the absence of treatment. Test: compare pre-treatment trends; placebo tests using pre-treatment periods as 'fake' treatments.

whenWhy.title: Instrumental variables: exploiting exogenous variation
whenWhy.body: IV estimation: find an instrument that (1) affects the treatment (relevance), (2) affects the outcome only through the treatment (exclusion restriction), (3) is independent of unmeasured confounders. The IV estimator is the ratio of the reduced form effect (instrument on outcome) to the first stage effect (instrument on treatment). It estimates the Local Average Treatment Effect — the effect for those whose treatment status actually changes with the instrument. Classic instruments: draft lottery for military service, geographic distance to college for the effect of education on wages.

whenWhy.title: Regression discontinuity: exploiting cutoff rules
whenWhy.body: RDD: when treatment is assigned based on whether a running variable exceeds a threshold (e.g., scholarship for test score above 70), compare outcomes just above and below the cutoff. Identification relies on the assumption that individuals just above and below the cutoff are similar in all other respects — they only differ in whether they received treatment. Sharp RDD: deterministic assignment. Fuzzy RDD: probabilistic — use RDD as a first stage in IV.

deepDiveIntro: In the potential outcomes framework, each unit has a potential outcome under treatment and a potential outcome under control. The observed outcome is whichever one corresponds to the treatment actually received. The Average Treatment Effect (ATE) is the expected difference between these two potential outcomes. Under unconfoundedness — all confounders are measured — the ATE is identified by regression adjustment or propensity score methods.

SUTVA (Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption) requires two things: no interference (one unit's outcome depends only on its own treatment, not others') and consistency (the potential outcome under the observed treatment equals the observed outcome). Both are often violated in practice — network effects violate no interference, and treatment variation (different doses) violates consistency.

deepDive.heading: Difference-in-Differences
deepDive.body: The DiD model adds an interaction term between a treatment indicator and a post-treatment indicator to a linear regression. The coefficient on this interaction is the DiD estimator of the ATE. The identifying assumption is that the two groups were on parallel trends before treatment — that the counterfactual trend for the treated group would have matched the control group's trend. This can be checked by examining pre-treatment trends. Recent work (Callaway & Sant'Anna 2021, Goodman-Bacon 2021) showed that staggered treatment timing — where different units are treated at different times — invalidates the standard two-way fixed effects estimator. [1]

deepDive.heading: Instrumental Variables (2SLS)
deepDive.body: Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS): Stage 1 regresses the treatment on the instrument and other controls to get predicted treatment values. Stage 2 regresses the outcome on the predicted treatment values and controls. The 2SLS estimate is consistent for the Local Average Treatment Effect (LATE) — the effect for compliers, those who change their treatment in response to the instrument. Weak instruments (F-statistic below 10 in the first stage) cause severe small-sample bias toward OLS. The Cragg-Donald or Kleibergen-Paap F-statistic tests first-stage strength. The Sargan-Hansen over-identification test checks the exclusion restriction when multiple instruments are used. [1]
deepDive.formula: β_IV = Cov(Z, Y) / Cov(Z, X)  [LATE for compliers]

deepDive.heading: Regression Discontinuity Design
deepDive.body: Sharp RDD estimates the treatment effect exactly at the assignment threshold by comparing outcomes just above and below it. This is estimated via local linear regression on each side of the cutoff, with a bandwidth chosen to balance bias and variance. The triangular kernel gives more weight to observations closer to the cutoff. Validity tests include: the McCrary density test (no bunching at the cutoff, which would suggest manipulation), covariate continuity tests (pre-determined characteristics should not jump at the cutoff), and placebo tests using fake cutoffs. [1]

deepDive.heading: Propensity Score Matching
deepDive.body: Under unconfoundedness (treatment is independent of potential outcomes given observed covariates), the propensity score — the probability of treatment given covariates — is a sufficient summary of the covariates for removing confounding. Matching treated and control units with similar propensity scores gives a consistent estimator of the ATT (Average Treatment Effect on the Treated). Doubly robust estimators (AIPW — Augmented Inverse Probability Weighting) are consistent if either the propensity score model or the outcome model is correctly specified, providing robustness against model misspecification. [1]

takeaways: ATE = E[Y(1)−Y(0)]; identified under unconfoundedness by regression adjustment or propensity score. [1]
DiD: τ = (ȳ_treat,post−ȳ_treat,pre) − (ȳ_ctrl,post−ȳ_ctrl,pre); requires parallel trends assumption. [1]
IV: β_IV = Cov(Z,Y)/Cov(Z,X); estimates LATE for compliers; weak instruments bias toward OLS. [1]
RDD: compares outcomes just above/below assignment threshold; local linear regression with optimal bandwidth. [1]
Doubly robust (AIPW): consistent if either propensity score or outcome model is correctly specified. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between ATE, ATT, and LATE?
edgeCases.a: ATE is the average treatment effect over the full population. ATT is the average effect over only those who were treated — what matters if the treatment would only be applied to similar people as currently treated. LATE is the effect only for compliers (those whose treatment status changes with the instrument) — not necessarily the same as ATE or ATT. These estimands can differ substantially when treatment effects are heterogeneous. [1]

edgeCases.q: When does the parallel trends assumption fail in DiD?
edgeCases.a: Parallel trends fails when: (1) the treatment is endogenous — treated units were already on a different trend pre-treatment (Ashenfelter's dip); (2) there are spillovers — control units are affected by the treatment (SUTVA violation); (3) the composition of groups changes over time (attrition, migration); (4) anticipation effects — treated units change behavior before treatment. Pre-treatment trend tests (event studies) check parallel trends before treatment but cannot test the post-treatment counterfactual. Synthetic control (Abadie et al.) constructs a weighted control group that matches the treated unit's pre-trends. [1]

sources.label: Angrist & Pischke — Mostly Harmless Econometrics, Ch. 3–6
Pearl — Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference (2nd ed.)
